data.title: Alaska Air expects Q2 EPS loss of about $1 while suspending full-year guide amid fuel volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alaska Air Group (ALK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Air Group reported a first quarter GAAP net loss of $193 million. Excluding special items, Air Group reported an adjusted net loss of $192 million.” (Vice President of Finance, Planning & Investor Relations Ryan St. John)

  • “Fuel costs were more than $100 million higher in the first quarter, and we expect incremental fuel costs of $600 million or more in the second quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Benito Minicucci) “From an integration standpoint, we've completed preparations for our single passenger service system cutover, our final major guest-facing milestone.” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci)

  • “Total Q1 revenues reached $3.3 billion, up 5% year-over-year on capacity growth of just 1.7%.” (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Andrew Harrison) “In response, we've reduced Puerto Vallarta flying by approximately 30% in the second quarter to better align capacity with demand.” (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison)

  • “Against that backdrop, our first quarter adjusted loss per share of $1.68 came in better than the midpoint of our revised guidance, reflecting both the resilience of demand and the discipline with which we're managing the business.” (CFO & Executive VP of Finance Shane Tackett)

Outlook

  • “These assumptions result in an EPS estimate of a loss of approximately $1 per share.” (CFO Tackett) That compares to analysts’ Q2 2026 EPS estimate of (-$0.11).

  • “Given how dynamic the current fuel price and demand backdrop are, we are suspending our full year guide until conditions stabilize and we have better line of sight to earnings beyond the current quarter.” (CFO Tackett)

  • “We now expect second quarter capacity to be up approximately 1% year-over-year, again, among the lowest growth rates in the industry, comprised entirely of our long-haul international service out of Seattle, while our North America capacity is down slightly year-over-year.” (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison)

  • “Assuming continued strength in demand where the balance of bookings that come during the quarter are at currently observed yields, we expect a path to unit revenues of 10%.” (CFO Tackett) “And for fuel... we would put the quarter average at $4.50 per gallon.” (CFO Tackett)

Financial Results

  • Total Q1 revenue was $3.3B (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison) vs. analysts’ Q1 2026 revenue estimate was not provided in the prompt’s estimates JSON.

  • Q1 adjusted loss per share was (-$1.68). (CFO Tackett) Air Group also reported a Q1 GAAP net loss of $193M and an adjusted net loss of $192M. (Vice President St. John)

  • “We have approximately $2.9 billion of total liquidity, including cash on hand and our undrawn line of credit and $20 billion in unencumbered assets.” (CFO Tackett) “During the quarter, we repaid $340 million of debt.” (CFO Tackett)

  • “Given the dislocation in our share price in March and April, our share repurchases accelerated, bringing our year-to-date total to $250 million... We have $180 million remaining under our $1 billion authorization, but we'll pause further repurchases to evaluate the outlook for the remainder of the year.” (CFO Tackett)

Q&A

  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: Asked for “core RASM... without the synergies and initiatives”; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison: “It's probably a couple of points.”

  • Conor Cunningham, Melius: Pressed on second-half costs and the long-term $10 EPS target; CFO Tackett: “I think there's 3 to 4 points in the second quarter that are not really structural to the business”; President, CEO & Director Minicucci: “I will tell you, if it's not '27, it's coming.”

  • Andrew Didora, BofA: Asked about demand elasticity and consolidation; Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison: “there is absolutely elasticity”; President, CEO & Director Minicucci: “consolidation can only happen... it's got to be pro-consumer and pro-competitive.”

  • Scott Group, Wolfe: Asked if higher fares could be “stickier” after fuel normalizes; CFO Tackett: “we believe that there's a lot of reasons that it could be different this time.”

  • Thomas Fitzgerald, TD Cowen: Asked about cadence of the Bank of America deal economics; CFO Tackett: “it's roughly 0.5 point of margin this year and a full point of margin next year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around durability of fares, the $10 EPS timeline, and visibility amid fuel volatility, including questions like “Do you think it's different this time?” (Scott Group, Wolfe) and “what gives you so much conviction on this $10 figure long term?” (Conor Cunningham, Melius).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in strategic conviction but more defensive on macro uncertainty, including “we are suspending our full year guide” (CFO Tackett) alongside “I've never been more convicted. Things are working.” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci).

  • Versus Q4 2025, management language shifted from guided confidence (“We expect full year earnings per share to be in the range of $3.50 to $6.50.” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci, Q4 2025)) to near-term caution tied to fuel volatility (“the range of potential financial outcomes remains wide and difficult to predict.” (CFO Tackett)).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture changed from Q4 2025 full-year EPS guidance (“$3.50 to $6.50” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci, Q4 2025)) to Q1 2026 suspension of full-year guidance (“we are suspending our full year guide” (CFO Tackett)).

  • Capital return messaging shifted from continued buybacks in Q4 2025 (“We expect to continue to execute share repurchases in 2026 to at least offset dilution.” (CFO Tackett, Q4 2025)) to a Q1 2026 pause (“we'll pause further repurchases” (CFO Tackett)).

  • Integration narrative progressed from Q4 2025 planning for April PSS operational cutover (“operational cutover scheduled for April of this year.” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci, Q4 2025)) to Q1 2026 execution timing (“Beginning tomorrow, our systems will operate on a single platform.” (President, CEO & Director Minicucci)).

Risks and Concerns

  • “Fuel has moved sharply higher and remains volatile.” (CFO Tackett) “In just the past 7 days, fuel prices have moved to as high as $5.15 per gallon and as low as $4.45.” (CFO Tackett)

  • Weather and geopolitical disruptions were cited as demand headwinds: “unprecedented storms... disrupted travel plans” in Hawaii and “civil unrest” in Puerto Vallarta. (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison)

  • “Fuel sourced from Singapore... became the most expensive, impacting roughly 20% of our total consumption.” (CFO Tackett) Management also referenced longer-term West Coast Jet A supply challenges: “we need to work on the West Coast Jet A supply issue long term.” (CFO Tackett)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 results around fuel-driven pressure and transitory network shocks while emphasizing integration progress and revenue-mix initiatives under Alaska Accelerate, including the imminent single passenger service system cutover, premium retrofit benefits, loyalty momentum, and a Bank of America extension that management said “secures an additional $1 billion of total cash remuneration” through 2030 (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Harrison). For Q2, management provided an EPS loss expectation of about $1 and suspended full-year guidance due to fuel volatility, while also outlining capacity discipline and continued focus on premium, loyalty, and international expansion out of Seattle.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comfort Systems rated Buy at GLJ on data-center boom
data.description:
GLJ Research initiated coverage of Comfort Systems USA (FIX) with a Buy rating and a $2,001 price target, arguing the mechanical and electrical contractor is well positioned to capitalize on surging demand tied to data center construction and modular cooling systems. The target implies about 21% upside from the stock price cited in the report.

Research analyst Austin Wang said Comfort Systems (FIX) stands out as a “best-in-class capacity provider” with strong backlog growth, rising margins and the ability to add capacity as customers expand projects.

Data centers drive bullish thesis

The report highlighted acute demand for cooling systems used in data centers, along with a shortage of skilled labor needed to install them. GLJ said those conditions favor larger operators such as Comfort Systems (FIX) that can scale labor and execute large projects.

The firm also pointed to Comfort Systems’ ownership of Kodiak Labor Solutions as a competitive advantage, giving the company access to workers in a tight labor market.

GLJ said the company’s presence in major data center regions such as Northern Virginia, Texas, Indiana, Arizona and Washington should help it win additional business.

Estimates above Wall Street

GLJ forecasts 2028 revenue and earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization above current Wall Street expectations. The firm projects 2028 revenue of $14.8 billion and ebitda of $2.56 billion, representing increases of 7.1% and 7.7%, respectively, versus consensus estimates cited in the report.

The report also models continued operating leverage, with revenue growth outpacing costs as expanding demand absorbs new capacity.

Premium valuation backed by growth

GLJ valued Comfort Systems at 27.4 times projected 2028 ebitda, above a peer median multiple of 24.9 times. The premium reflects what the firm described as continued high-teens ebitda growth, disciplined capital allocation and an attractive position outside the more crowded transmission and distribution contractor space.

The report cited several risks, including fixed-price contract exposure, execution challenges and customer concentration or reputation issues.

data.title: Navy turns to Patriot missiles for ship defense in boost for Lockheed
data.description:
The U.S. Navy plans to equip warships with Patriot missile interceptors in a move aimed at strengthening defenses against drones and incoming missiles, according to officials Tuesday, broadening the role of a weapon system long associated with land-based air defense.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) said it received an initial contract valued at about $93 million to integrate the Patriot missile into a naval weapons architecture, with the total program potentially reaching roughly $200 million over time.

The Navy is also seeking $1.7 billion in its fiscal 2027 budget request to purchase the PAC-3 Missile Segment Enhancement, or PAC-3 MSE, Lockheed’s most advanced Patriot interceptor, according to Pentagon procurement documents.

The development signals expanding demand for missile-defense systems at a time when geopolitical tensions are driving higher military spending. For investors, the move underscores a favorable backdrop for defense contractors such as Lockheed Martin (LMT) and RTX (RTX), particularly in high-margin precision munitions and interceptors where replenishment needs are rising quickly. It also suggests multi-year revenue visibility as the Pentagon commits to larger weapons inventories.

The initiative comes as drone and missile attacks in the Persian Gulf have highlighted the strain modern conflicts can place on interceptor stockpiles, forcing militaries to replenish expensive but critical defensive munitions.

The Navy’s decision follows several years of testing that demonstrated Patriot missiles could be successfully paired with the Navy’s Aegis Combat System, the radar-and-targeting network used aboard cruisers and destroyers.

Patriot systems have historically been deployed by the U.S. Army and allied nations as a ground-based shield against aircraft and missile threats. By adding the interceptor to naval inventories, the Pentagon is expanding flexibility across branches while deepening available missile reserves.

Rear Admiral Ben Reynolds, the Navy’s deputy assistant secretary for budget, said the effort is about “pulling every lever” to increase “magazine depth,” referring to the number of munitions available to combat commanders. Existing naval arsenals already include the Standard Missile-6 and Tomahawk cruise missile.

Production is already being scaled higher. The Pentagon previously awarded Lockheed (LMT) a seven-year agreement intended to increase annual PAC-3 MSE output from roughly 600 missiles to 2,000 by the end of 2030.

The Army’s own five-year spending outlook points to even stronger Patriot demand. Budget documents show plans to spend more than $12.2 billion on upgraded Patriot interceptors, with most of that funded through mandatory spending measures.

For fiscal 2027 alone, the Army expects to buy about 250 PAC-3 MSE missiles for nearly $1.3 billion through discretionary funding, while a separate mandatory package would fund more than 2,500 additional missiles at a cost of $10.9 billion.

Meanwhile, the Navy projected purchases of RTX’s (RTX) Tomahawk missiles will surge to 785 next year from just 55 this year, another sign the Pentagon is rapidly rebuilding and expanding missile inventories.

data.title: TMX Group Limited reports FY results
data.description:
• TMX Group Limited press release (X:CA): FY Revenue of $4.7M.
  • 40% gross margin, reflecting strong operating leverage
  • 23% franchise network growth in 2025

  • Record contract growth and backlog in Q3 2025

data.title: Is BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP trend reversal here? Bitcoin leads $1.48B inflows
data.description:
Cryptocurrency investment products saw strong inflows of ~$1.4B last week, led by Bitcoin (BTC-USD) with ~$1.116M inflows, taking YTD inflows to ~$3.1B.

The overall reported inflows of the prior week were the highest since January and also marked the third straight week of positive flows, showing continued global interest.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) inflows were mainly driven by the most awaited price move, as the asset recently surged above ~$76K after two months of sideways movement. Short-Bitcoin products also saw small inflows of ~$1.4M.

On the other hand, Ethereum (ETH-USD) also saw strong demand with ~$328M inflows, its best week since January, which brings its YTD inflows to ~$197M.

Meanwhile, XRP (XRP-USD) saw outflows of ~$56M, and Solana (SOL-USD) saw small outflows of ~US$2.3M.

According to CoinShares Head of Research James Butterfill, this trend shows rising risk appetite. He linked mixed signals to US–Iran ceasefire extension talks and BTC crossing ~$76K.

He also said March CPI at 3.3% YoY was mostly ignored by the market, while core CPI at 2.6% suggests inflation is coming more from supply issues, not from broad price pressure.

As per the reports, the US led all regions with ~$1.5B inflows, while Germany saw ~$28M inflows. However, Switzerland recorded ~$138M in outflows, the biggest since November, going against the overall positive trend.

According to the SoSoValue chart, as of April 20, US spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded ~$238M net inflows, marking the 5th straight day of inflows. US spot Ethereum ETFs also saw ~$67.77M inflows, marking the 8th regular day of positive flows.

Overall, the weekly ETF data showed strong and improving investor confidence in crypto markets, with fresh buying interest in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) from major market mover companies like Strategy (MSTR), Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), and BlackRock (BLK).

data.title: Microsoft perks up as Citi sees 'solid checks' going into Q3 results
data.description:
• Microsoft (MSFT) shares rose nearly 2% on Tuesday as investment firm Citi said it sees “solid checks” going into the tech giant's fiscal third-quarter results. 
  • “We are incrementally more positive into MSFT's F3Q, with upbeat takeaways from our reseller survey, AI Summit, Fabric conference, and intra-Q NDR,” analyst Tyler Radke wrote in a note to clients. “Investor expectations and sentiment are relatively low, and there are near-term constraints on Azure/O365 growth, we believe fundamentals are improving and should yield a desirable accelerating growth story throughout FY27, where Citi's Azure estimate is 3 points ahead of consensus. Our reseller checks improved QoQ against mixed partner checks. While our CIO survey was more positive for MSFT, amidst tightening budgets showing strong AI mindshare.”
  • Radke reiterated his Buy rating on Microsoft, but lowered his price target to $600 from $635, citing multiple compression.
  • Looking ahead, Radke upped his capex forecasts for both the fiscal fourth-quarter and the first quarter of fiscal 2027, citing Office 365 growth. “MSFT remains a top megacap idea,” Radke added.
  • Microsoft is set to report fiscal third-quarter results on April 29 after the close of trading. A consensus of analysts expects the company to earn $4.07 per share on $81.39B in revenue.

data.title: Morgan Stanley’s Katy Huberty sees tactical pause to European equities’ rebound
data.description:
Investors should “expect choppiness this earnings season” for European equities (VGK), (IEV), according to Katy L. Huberty, global director of research at Morgan Stanley.

While the firm remains constructive on the medium-term outlook, Huberty anticipates a tactical pause to the rebound at the index level as investor sentiment has become overextended.

The strategist points to elevated expectations around geopolitical developments as a key factor behind the expected pause.

According to Huberty, investor sentiment, “particularly around expectations for a resolution to ongoing disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, has come a long way,” suggesting the market may have priced in too optimistic a scenario.

Despite the anticipated choppiness at the index level, Huberty sees favorable conditions emerging for individual stock selection.

 

 

 

The strategist notes that consensus earnings growth for MSCI Europe (IEUR) overall is moving up, and stock-level dispersion is currently “well above seasonal averages,” making for a “strong stock-picking environment” as the market shifts back to idiosyncratic drivers amid earnings season.

Looking at sector-specific expectations, Morgan Stanley identifies clear winners and losers heading into the reporting period.

According to the strategist, the firm expects “the biggest skew to beats in the energy, utilities, banks, and telecom sectors,” positioning these industries as potential outperformers during the current earnings cycle.

Conversely, Huberty warns that certain sectors face a more challenging earnings hurdle.

The strategist expects “the biggest skew to misses in luxury, autos, and staples,” according to Morgan Stanley’s analysis, suggesting investors may want to approach these industries with greater caution as companies report their quarterly results.

European-focused ETFs: (VGK), (EZU), (BBEU), (IEUR), (HEDJ), (IEV), (FDD), (SPEU), and (FEP).

data.title: Ball upgraded, Graphic Packaging downgraded at Raymond James
data.description:
Ball Corp. (BALL) won a vote of confidence from Raymond James on Tuesday after analyst Matt Roberts upgraded the shares to Outperform from Market Perform, arguing the company’s beverage can business continues to benefit from durable industry trends.

Roberts said beverage cans appear better insulated than other packaging categories from broader demand weakness tied to inflation and shifting consumer spending.

“We believe BevCans are particularly immune to broader volume concerns in other substrates/categories or, at the least, relatively less impacted by potential indirect demand impacts from inflation,” Roberts wrote in a note to clients.

The analyst also pointed to Ball’s relatively modest exposure to Asia and the Middle East, potentially limiting geopolitical and regional demand risks compared with more globally exposed industrial names.

In addition, Roberts said higher input costs for aluminum, energy and freight are largely passed through to customers on a spot basis, helping protect margins even in a volatile cost environment.

Raymond James set a $73 price target on Ball (BALL) shares, implying roughly 14% upside from the stock’s previous close.

Graphic Packaging cut as pricing power wanes

In the same sector call, Raymond James downgraded Graphic Packaging Holding (GPK) to Underperform from Market Perform.

Roberts said paper-based packaging markets remain challenged, with weak supply-demand dynamics limiting the company’s ability to raise prices.

“Applicable paper substrates have lacked any pricing power to date given unfavorable supply and demand conditions,” he wrote.

The contrasting calls suggest Raymond James sees a more favorable setup in metal packaging, where pricing structures and category resilience may offer steadier returns than paper packaging markets facing oversupply pressure.

data.title: OpenAI launches Images 2.0, allowing users to generate multiple images at once
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) introduced ChatGPT Images 2.0 on Tuesday, which improves on the quality of the images, reduces the error rate, and allows users to generate multiple images at the same time.

"Images 2.0 is a huge step forward," said OpenAI CEO Sam Altman during a Tuesday livestream. "It's like going from GPT-3 to GPT-5 all at once. Its ability to make extremely beautiful things is remarkable. The team really cooked with this one, and we can't wait to see what you'll do with it."

The model is more deliberate with where it places texts and images in the design, OpenAI said. There is an Instant and a Thinking version of the model.

"When a thinking model is selected in ChatGPT, Images 2.0 can search the web for real-time information, create multiple distinct images from one prompt, and double-check its own outputs," the company said. "With thinking, the model can take on even more of the heavy lifting between idea and image, especially when accuracy, up-to-date information, consistency, and visual cohesion matter most."

Images 2.0 is available today for all users in ChatGPT, Codex, and the API.

data.title: Trump nominates Christopher Phelan to head the Council of Economic Advisers
data.description:
President Donald Trump nominated Christopher Phelan to be the chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, replacing Stephen Miran, who resigned when his stint as a Federal Reserve governor extended past February.

Phelan is a  professor of economics at the University of Minnesota and an adviser to the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis's Research Department, the district bank said on its website.

The administration submitted Phelan's nomination on Tuesday, according to a statement.

Phelan received his AB and AM degrees in economics and computer science from Duke University and the University of Chicago. In 1990, he received a PhD in economics from the University of Chicago. His research agenda focuses on dynamic contracting, government reputation, game theory, and macroeconomics, the Minneapolis Fed said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
